C:
Use a monorepo. For a 20-engineer startup, it's the stronger default, and the cases where multi-repo wins mostly don't apply at your size yet.

## Why monorepo fits you

**Coordination cost is your real bottleneck, not scale.** At 20 engineers everyone's work touches everyone else's. A monorepo lets you make a cross-cutting change — rename an API, bump a shared type, update a client — in a single atomic commit and PR. With multiple repos that same change becomes a choreographed sequence of versioned releases across repos, and you spend real time on coordination overhead that buys you nothing at this size.

**Atomic changes across service boundaries.** When the backend contract and the frontend consumer live in one repo, a breaking change and its fix land together, get reviewed together, and CI tests them together. Multi-repo forces you to version internal interfaces and manage compatibility windows — that's infrastructure you'd be maintaining for a team small enough to just talk to each other.

**One source of truth for tooling.** A single lint config, test setup, CI pipeline, and dependency version. New hires clone one thing and are productive. In a multi-repo world, tooling drifts repo by repo, and "which version of the shared lib is this repo on" becomes a recurring tax.

**Visibility and code reuse.** Shared code is just an import, not a publish-and-version cycle. Everyone can see and grep the whole system, which matters a lot when the team is still building shared context.

## What you're trading away

The honest costs, and why they're manageable now:

- **CI gets slower as the repo grows.** Mitigate with a build tool that does affected-only builds and caching (Turborepo or Nx for JS/TS, Bazel if you're polyglot and willing to invest). You likely don't need this on day one, but adopt it before CI times hurt.
- **Looser access control.** A monorepo is mostly all-or-nothing on read access. If you don't have a hard requirement to wall off a codebase (regulated component, contractors with limited scope), this isn't a problem yet.
- **Accidental coupling.** Easy import means easy tangle. Enforce module boundaries with ownership rules (CODEOWNERS) and lint-level dependency constraints so the structure doesn't rot.

## When I'd reconsider

Split a repo out when there's a *concrete* reason, not preemptively:

- A component with genuinely different security or compliance isolation needs.
- Open-source code you publish externally.
- A mature, stable service with its own independent release cadence and a dedicated team — a situation you'll grow into, not start with.

The reversible direction matters here: starting in one monorepo and extracting a service later is a routine, well-understood operation. Starting with ten repos and consolidating is a painful migration. Pick the option that keeps your future choices open, and that's the monorepo.

My recommendation: one monorepo, add affected-only CI tooling when build times start to pinch, and enforce ownership boundaries from the start so it stays navigable.

**Coordination cost is your real bottleneck, not scale.** At 20 engineers everyone's work touches everyone else's. A monorepo lets you make a cross-cutting change — rename an API, bump a shared type, update a client — in a single atomic commit and PR. With multiple repos that same change becomes a choreographed sequence of versioned releases across repos, and you spend real time on coordination overhead that buys you nothing at this size.

Multi-repo forces you to version internal interfaces and manage compatibility windows — that's infrastructure you'd be maintaining for a team small enough to just talk to each other.

**Visibility and code reuse.** Shared code is just an import, not a publish-and-version cycle. Everyone can see and grep the whole system, which matters a lot when the team is still building shared context.

## What you're trading away

The honest costs, and why they're manageable now:

- **CI gets slower as the repo grows.** Mitigate with a build tool that does affected-only builds and caching (Turborepo or Nx for JS/TS, Bazel if you're polyglot and willing to invest). You likely don't need this on day one, but adopt it before CI times hurt.
- **Looser access control.** A monorepo is mostly all-or-nothing on read access. If you don't have a hard requirement to wall off a codebase (regulated component, contractors with limited scope), this isn't a problem yet.
- **Accidental coupling.** Easy import means easy tangle. Enforce module boundaries with ownership rules (CODEOWNERS) and lint-level dependency constraints so the structure doesn't rot.

## When I'd reconsider

Split a repo out when there's a *concrete* reason, not preemptively:

- A component with genuinely different security or compliance isolation needs.
- Open-source code you publish externally.
- A mature, stable service with its own independent release cadence and a dedicated team — a situation you'll grow into, not start with.

The reversible direction matters here: starting in one monorepo and extracting a service later is a routine, well-understood operation. Starting with ten repos and consolidating is a painful migration. Pick the option that keeps your future choices open, and that's the monorepo.

**What would change my answer.** This recommendation rests on you being one mostly-co-located team sharing one or two primary language stacks, building tightly-coupled internal services, with no hard isolation requirement. Flip any of those and the call can flip: if you already run several genuinely independent products with separate teams and release cadences, if a regulated or customer-isolated component legally can't share a read boundary, if large chunks are open-source you publish externally, or if your stacks are so divergent that no single build/CI tool serves them well — then multi-repo (or a hybrid: monorepo core plus a few carved-out repos) becomes the better fit. The main limitation to watch is that monorepo benefits depend on disciplined tooling (affected-only CI, enforced module boundaries); without that investment the costs above show up faster than the payoff, and that's the condition under which I'd revisit this.

My recommendation: one monorepo, add affected-only CI tooling when build times start to pinch, and enforce ownership boundaries from the start so it stays navigable.
P:
Use a monorepo. For a 20-engineer startup it's the stronger default: the problems a monorepo solves (coordination overhead) are the ones you have now, and the problems it creates (scale, isolation) are ones you don't have yet — and may never grow into.

One assumption worth stating up front, because it's the main thing that would change the answer: this holds if your team works in one or a few closely-related stacks sharing code and contracts. If you're really three separate worlds — native mobile, a Go backend, and a Python data/ML team that share almost nothing — the unification benefits below mostly evaporate, and per-stack repos can be cleaner. Most 20-person startups aren't that; if yours is, see "When I'd reconsider."

## Why it fits you

**Cross-cutting changes stay atomic.** At 20 engineers, most work touches a shared boundary. Renaming an API, bumping a shared type, or changing a contract and its consumer is one commit, one PR, one CI run — reviewed and tested together. In multi-repo that same change becomes a sequence of versioned releases with compatibility windows between them. That's release engineering you'd be maintaining for a team small enough to just talk to each other.

**One source of truth for tooling and onboarding.** A single lint config, test setup, CI pipeline, and dependency graph. A new hire clones one thing and is productive. Across many repos, tooling drifts repo by repo and "which version of the shared lib is this one on?" becomes a recurring tax. Shared code is an import, not a publish-and-version cycle — and everyone can grep the whole system, which matters most while the team is still building shared context.

**It keeps your options open.** This is the decisive one. Starting in a monorepo and later extracting a service to its own repo is routine and well-understood. Starting with ten repos and consolidating is a painful migration. When the reversible direction is this asymmetric, pick the side you can cheaply back out of.

## What you're trading away, and why it's manageable now

- **CI slows as the repo grows.** Fix it when it starts to pinch, not on day one: adopt a build tool with affected-only builds and caching — Turborepo or Nx for JS/TS, Bazel if you're polyglot and willing to invest in it. Adopt before build times hurt, not after.
- **Coarse access control.** A monorepo is largely all-or-nothing on read access. Only a problem if you have a hard requirement to wall off code (a regulated component, scoped contractors). If you don't, you don't.
- **Accidental coupling.** Easy imports make easy tangles. Counter it from the start with CODEOWNERS and lint-level dependency constraints so module boundaries are enforced, not just hoped for.

## When I'd reconsider — and split a repo out

Split for a concrete reason, never preemptively:

- A component with genuinely different security or compliance isolation needs.
- Code you publish or open-source externally.
- A mature, stable service with its own release cadence and a dedicated owning team — something you grow into, not start with.
- The polyglot caveat above: teams that share no code, no contracts, and no tooling get little from co-location.

## Concrete first steps

1. One monorepo, with a clear top-level directory structure by domain or service.
2. CODEOWNERS and lint-enforced module boundaries from day one — this is what keeps it navigable at 50 engineers, not 20.
3. Add affected-only CI tooling (Turborepo / Nx / Bazel) when build times start to bite, not before.

Revisit the structure when a real isolation, publishing, or independent-cadence need shows up — and then extract just that piece.
